items.title: GST में खरीददार को इनपुट क्रेडिट सुरक्षा की खबर: सपना, प्रस्ताव और संभावना
items.summary: क्या जीएसटी में ईमानदार खरीदार का इनपुट टैक्स क्रेडिट (ITC) अब अधिक सुरक्षित होगा? जानिए खरीदार की ITC सुरक्षा से जुड़े प्रस्ताव, GSTR-2B की संभावित भूमिका, विक्रेता द्वारा टैक्स जमा न करने की स्थिति, पुराने विवादों पर संभावित प्रभाव और जीएसटी कानून में प्रस्तावित बदलाव से जुड़ी प्रमुख बातें। The post GST में खरीददार को इनपुट क्रेडिट सुरक्षा की खबर: सपना, प्रस्ताव और संभावना appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), has undergone numerous amendments and clarifications since its inception. One of the critical aspects of GST is the availability of input tax credit (ITC) to registered persons. Section 16 of the CGST Act governs the eligibility and conditions for claiming ITC, while Section 41 deals with the claim of credit and Section 42 provides for the matching of input tax credit. The concept of ITC is fundamental to the GST framework, as it allows businesses to reduce their tax liability by claiming credit for the taxes paid on inputs used in the course of their business.

The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, has been instrumental in addressing the challenges faced by taxpayers and making recommendations to the government for amendments to the GST laws. The Council's efforts have led to several changes aimed at simplifying the GST framework and reducing compliance burdens. For instance, the introduction of the GST Returns filing system, including the GSTR-2B, has been a significant step towards improving the ITC claim process. GSTR-2B is an auto-drafted input tax credit statement that reflects the eligible ITC based on the supplies reported by the suppliers in their GSTR-1.

Despite these efforts, the issue of ITC availability and its security has remained a contentious issue. The GST law provides for certain conditions and restrictions on the claim of ITC, such as the requirement that the supplier should have paid the tax, the recipient should have received the goods or services, and the tax invoice or debit note should have been issued. However, in cases where the supplier fails to pay the tax or the recipient is unable to claim the ITC due to mismatch or other issues, the buyer's ITC becomes insecure. This insecurity can lead to additional tax liabilities, interest, and even penalties for the buyer, even though they have acted in good faith and complied with all the requirements.

The Development in Detail

Recently, there have been proposals and discussions around enhancing the security of ITC for honest buyers. The idea is to protect the buyers from the consequences of the supplier's default, such as non-payment of tax. This proposal is based on the principle that a buyer should not be penalized for the actions of the supplier, as long as the buyer has complied with all the requirements and has acted in good faith. The proposal suggests that in cases where the supplier fails to pay the tax, the buyer should not be denied the ITC, and instead, the supplier should be held liable for the tax and any related penalties.

The role of GSTR-2B in this context is crucial, as it provides a mechanism for the buyer to verify the eligibility of ITC and ensure that the supplier has reported the supplies and paid the tax. If the supplier fails to report the supplies or pay the tax, the GSTR-2B will reflect the mismatch, and the buyer can take corrective action to ensure that the ITC is not claimed improperly. The proposal to enhance ITC security for buyers is likely to involve amendments to the GST law, including changes to Sections 16, 41, and 42 of the CGST Act.

The proposed changes are expected to have a significant impact on the GST landscape, particularly for businesses that rely heavily on ITC to reduce their tax liability. The changes will provide greater certainty and security for buyers, allowing them to claim ITC without fear of denial or penalty due to the supplier's default. However, the proposal also raises questions about the potential impact on suppliers, who may face increased liability and penalties for non-payment of tax.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The proposal to enhance ITC security for buyers is likely to involve several key provisions and clarifications. Firstly, the GST law may be amended to provide that the buyer's ITC claim will not be denied if the supplier fails to pay the tax, as long as the buyer has complied with all the requirements and has acted in good faith. Secondly, the law may be clarified to provide that the supplier will be held liable for the tax and any related penalties in cases where the supplier fails to pay the tax.

The role of GSTR-2B will be critical in this context, as it will provide a mechanism for the buyer to verify the eligibility of ITC and ensure that the supplier has reported the supplies and paid the tax. The GST Council may also provide clarifications on the process for claiming ITC in cases where the supplier fails to pay the tax, including the documentation requirements and the timelines for claiming the credit. Additionally, the Council may provide guidance on the consequences for suppliers who fail to pay the tax, including the penalties and interest that may be applicable.

The proposed changes will also have implications for the existing disputes and litigation related to ITC claims. The amendments may provide a mechanism for resolving pending disputes and may also have a bearing on the interpretation of the GST law by the courts. For instance, the Supreme Court's decision in the case of Union of India vs. VKC Footsteps India Private Limited (2020) may be relevant in this context, as it dealt with the issue of ITC eligibility and the requirements for claiming credit.

Critical Analysis

The proposal to enhance ITC security for buyers is a welcome move, as it will provide greater certainty and security for businesses that rely heavily on ITC to reduce their tax liability. However, the proposal also raises questions about the potential impact on suppliers, who may face increased liability and penalties for non-payment of tax. The amendments to the GST law will need to be carefully crafted to ensure that the interests of both buyers and suppliers are balanced.

The role of GSTR-2B will be critical in this context, as it will provide a mechanism for the buyer to verify the eligibility of ITC and ensure that the supplier has reported the supplies and paid the tax. However, the effectiveness of GSTR-2B will depend on the accuracy and completeness of the data reported by the suppliers. The GST Council will need to ensure that the system is robust and reliable, and that the data is accurate and up-to-date.

The proposed changes will also have implications for the compliance burden of businesses. The amendments may require businesses to maintain additional documentation and records to support their ITC claims, and may also require them to verify the eligibility of ITC through GSTR-2B. The GST Council will need to ensure that the compliance burden is minimized, and that the benefits of the proposed changes are realized by businesses.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The proposal to enhance ITC security for buyers will have significant compliance implications for businesses. To ensure that they are able to claim ITC without any issues, businesses will need to take the following steps:

1. Verify ITC eligibility through GSTR-2B: Businesses should verify the eligibility of ITC through GSTR-2B, to ensure that the supplier has reported the supplies and paid the tax.

2. Maintain accurate records: Businesses should maintain accurate and complete records of their ITC claims, including the tax invoices, debit notes, and credit notes.

3. Monitor supplier compliance: Businesses should monitor the compliance of their suppliers, to ensure that they are reporting the supplies and paying the tax.

4. Claim ITC in a timely manner: Businesses should claim ITC in a timely manner, to avoid any issues with the claim.

5. Seek professional advice: Businesses should seek professional advice, to ensure that they are complying with all the requirements and are able to claim ITC without any issues.

items.title: Board Meeting vs Circular Resolution under Companies Act, 2013
items.summary: Learn when a Board Meeting is mandatory and when a Circular Resolution can be used under the Companies Act, 2013. Explore the complete list of matters that must be approved only at a Board Meeting as per statutory provisions and Secretarial Standard-1 (SS-1). The post Board Meeting vs Circular Resolution under Companies Act, 2013 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Companies Act, 2013, is the primary legislation governing the incorporation, management, and operation of companies in India. The Act is complemented by the Secretarial Standard-1 (SS-1), which provides guidelines for the conduct of board meetings and passing of resolutions by circulation. Under the Companies Act, 2013, a company is required to hold board meetings at regular intervals to discuss and decide on various matters related to its management and operation. However, in certain circumstances, a company may pass resolutions by circulation, also known as circular resolutions, without the need for a board meeting.

Section 173 of the Companies Act, 2013, provides that every company shall hold a minimum number of board meetings in a calendar year, with a maximum gap of 120 days between two consecutive meetings. The section also empowers the board of directors to pass resolutions by circulation, subject to certain conditions. The Companies Act, 2013, and the SS-1 together provide a framework for the conduct of board meetings and the passing of circular resolutions, ensuring that companies are managed in a transparent and accountable manner.

The distinction between board meetings and circular resolutions is crucial, as it affects the validity and enforceability of decisions made by a company. While board meetings provide a platform for directors to discuss and debate matters, circular resolutions offer a convenient and efficient way to pass decisions without the need for a physical meeting. However, the use of circular resolutions is subject to certain limitations and requirements, which must be carefully observed to ensure compliance with the law.

The Development in Detail

The Companies Act, 2013, and the SS-1 provide a list of matters that must be approved only at a board meeting, and cannot be passed by circular resolution. These matters include the approval of financial statements, the appointment and removal of directors and key managerial personnel, and the declaration of dividends. The Act and the SS-1 also require that certain matters, such as the amendment of the company's articles of association and the alteration of the company's memorandum of association, be approved by a special resolution passed at a general meeting.

In addition to these specific requirements, the Companies Act, 2013, and the SS-1 impose certain general requirements for the passing of circular resolutions. For example, a circular resolution must be circulated to all directors, and a director must have a reasonable opportunity to comment on the proposal. The circular resolution must also be passed by a majority of the directors, and the company must maintain a record of the resolution and the comments received from directors.

The development of the law and practice in this area has been influenced by various court decisions and regulatory guidelines. For instance, the Supreme Court has held that a circular resolution passed in accordance with the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013, and the SS-1 is valid and binding on the company. The Court has also emphasized the importance of maintaining proper records and minutes of board meetings and circular resolutions, as required by Section 118 of the Companies Act, 2013.

Key Provisions & Impact

Section 179 of the Companies Act, 2013, provides that the board of directors of a company shall be responsible for exercising all the powers and functions of the company, except those that are reserved for the shareholders under the Act or the company's articles of association. The section also requires that the board shall exercise its powers and functions in accordance with the provisions of the Act and the company's articles of association. The provisions of Section 179 are crucial, as they define the scope of the board's authority and the manner in which it must exercise its powers.

The impact of the Companies Act, 2013, and the SS-1 on the governance and management of companies in India has been significant. The Act and the SS-1 have introduced greater transparency and accountability in the decision-making process, and have provided a framework for ensuring that companies are managed in a responsible and ethical manner. The provisions relating to board meetings and circular resolutions have also facilitated the efficient and effective management of companies, by allowing directors to pass decisions without the need for a physical meeting.

However, the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013, and the SS-1 also impose certain compliance requirements on companies, which must be carefully observed to avoid penalties and other consequences. For example, the failure to hold the required number of board meetings in a calendar year, or the failure to maintain proper records and minutes of board meetings and circular resolutions, can result in penalties and fines under the Act. Companies must therefore ensure that they comply with the provisions of the Act and the SS-1, and that they maintain proper records and documentation to demonstrate their compliance.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013, and the SS-1 reveals that they are designed to strike a balance between the need for efficient and effective decision-making, and the need for transparency and accountability. The provisions relating to board meetings and circular resolutions are intended to ensure that companies are managed in a responsible and ethical manner, and that directors are held accountable for their actions.

However, the provisions of the Act and the SS-1 can also be complex and difficult to navigate, particularly for small and medium-sized companies. The requirements for holding board meetings and passing circular resolutions can be time-consuming and costly, and may divert resources away from other important activities. Companies must therefore carefully consider their compliance obligations, and must seek professional advice and guidance to ensure that they comply with the provisions of the Act and the SS-1.

The development of the law and practice in this area is also influenced by various external factors, such as changes in the economic and regulatory environment. For instance, the introduction of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016, has had a significant impact on the governance and management of companies in India, and has highlighted the need for companies to maintain proper records and documentation to demonstrate their compliance with the law.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

Companies must ensure that they comply with the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013, and the SS-1, and must maintain proper records and documentation to demonstrate their compliance. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that the company holds the required number of board meetings in a calendar year, and that the meetings are properly convened and conducted.

2. Maintain proper records and minutes of board meetings and circular resolutions, as required by Section 118 of the Companies Act, 2013.

3. Ensure that circular resolutions are passed in accordance with the provisions of the Act and the SS-1, and that they are properly documented and recorded.

4. Review the company's articles of association and memorandum of association to ensure that they are up-to-date and compliant with the provisions of the Act.

5. Seek professional advice and guidance to ensure that the company complies with the provisions of the Act and the SS-1, and to address any complexities or difficulties that may arise.

items.title: Scope of Professional Practice Under GST Law in India
items.summary: Learn the scope of professional practice of GST Practitioners under the CGST Act and Rules, including eligibility, enrolment, authorised functions, return filing, refunds, registration, and representation before GST authorities. The post Scope of Professional Practice Under GST Law in India appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime in India, governed by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act), has ushered in a new era of indirect taxation. A critical component of this regime is the role of GST Practitioners, who are authorized to perform various functions under the law. The CGST Act, specifically Section 48, deals with the authorization of GST Practitioners, outlining their eligibility, enrollment process, and the scope of their professional practice. Understanding the statutory framework is essential to grasp the significance of GST Practitioners in the GST ecosystem.

Section 2(62) of the CGST Act defines a GST Practitioner as a person authorized to perform any or all of the following activities: filing of returns, furnishing details of outward and inward supplies, making payment of tax, and other such activities. This definition underscores the critical role GST Practitioners play in ensuring compliance with GST laws. Furthermore, the CGST Rules, specifically Rule 83, elaborate on the conditions for enrollment as a GST Practitioner, including the requirement for a valid PAN, a valid mobile number, and a valid email address, among others.

The legal framework governing GST Practitioners is also influenced by established precedents and circulars. For instance, the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) has issued circulars clarifying various aspects of GST Practitioner enrollment and their authorized functions. These circulars, while not altering the statutory provisions, provide valuable guidance on the operational aspects of GST Practitioner services. The interplay between the statutory provisions and these circulars is crucial for understanding the scope of professional practice under GST law.

The Development in Detail

The development in question pertains to the scope of professional practice of GST Practitioners under the CGST Act and Rules. This includes their eligibility, enrollment, authorized functions, return filing, refunds, registration, and representation before GST authorities. GST Practitioners are authorized to perform a range of activities, including filing of returns (GSTR-3 and GSTR-9), applying for registration, making amendments to registration particulars, and filing refund claims. The ability of GST Practitioners to undertake these tasks on behalf of taxpayers simplifies compliance and ensures that businesses can focus on their core activities.

The enrollment process for GST Practitioners involves applying on the GST portal, providing necessary documents, and undergoing a verification process. Once enrolled, GST Practitioners are issued a unique enrollment number, which they must use when performing any function under the Act. The enrollment is valid until it is surrendered or canceled, and GST Practitioners are required to maintain confidentiality and integrity in their professional practice. The CGST Act, through Section 122, also prescribes penalties for contravention of certain provisions by GST Practitioners, emphasizing the importance of ethical conduct.

The scope of professional practice of GST Practitioners is not limited to transactional compliance but also extends to advisory and representational services. They can provide guidance on GST law and procedures, assist in reconciliations, and represent taxpayers before the GST authorities in cases of audits, assessments, and appeals. This comprehensive role underscores the significance of GST Practitioners in the GST ecosystem, making them indispensable for taxpayers navigating the complexities of GST law.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Key provisions governing the scope of professional practice of GST Practitioners include Section 48 of the CGST Act, which deals with the authorization of GST Practitioners, and Rule 83 of the CGST Rules, which outlines the conditions for enrollment as a GST Practitioner. Additionally, Section 151 of the CGST Act empowers the Commissioner to prescribe the manner of approval of GST Practitioners, their eligibility conditions, and other related matters. These provisions, read together, provide a comprehensive framework for the regulation of GST Practitioners.

Clarifications issued by the CBIC have also provided guidance on aspects such as the process for surrender or cancellation of enrollment, the responsibilities of GST Practitioners, and the consequences of non-compliance. For instance, the CBIC has clarified that GST Practitioners are required to maintain records of the activities performed and that they are liable for any contravention of the provisions of the Act or the rules made thereunder. These clarifications are essential for ensuring that GST Practitioners understand their obligations and can provide effective services to taxpayers.

The legal framework governing GST Practitioners is also influenced by judicial precedents. While there may not be direct Supreme Court or High Court judgments specifically on GST Practitioners, the principles established in cases related to the role of tax professionals and the interpretation of tax laws are relevant. These precedents can provide guidance on issues such as the scope of professional practice, the duties of tax professionals, and the implications of non-compliance. Understanding these precedents is crucial for GST Practitioners to navigate the legal landscape effectively.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the scope of professional practice of GST Practitioners reveals that their role is multifaceted, extending beyond mere compliance to advisory and representational services. The statutory framework, coupled with clarifications and judicial precedents, underscores the importance of GST Practitioners in ensuring compliance with GST laws. However, the effectiveness of GST Practitioners in performing their roles depends on their understanding of the legal framework, their ability to navigate the GST portal, and their capacity to provide advisory services that add value to taxpayers.

The development also highlights the need for continuous education and training for GST Practitioners. Given the dynamic nature of tax laws, with frequent amendments and updates, GST Practitioners must stay abreast of the latest developments to provide effective services. This includes not only knowledge of the CGST Act and Rules but also an understanding of related laws such as the Income Tax Act, 1961, and the Companies Act, 2013, where relevant. Furthermore, the ethical conduct of GST Practitioners is paramount, as any breach of confidentiality or integrity can erode trust in the profession as a whole.

From a policy perspective, the regulation of GST Practitioners reflects the government's efforts to streamline tax compliance and administration. By authorizing GST Practitioners to perform various functions, the government aims to reduce the compliance burden on taxpayers, especially small and medium-sized enterprises. However, the success of this approach depends on the effective implementation of the regulatory framework, including the enforcement of standards for GST Practitioners and the provision of necessary infrastructure and support.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The scope of professional practice of GST Practitioners has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and practitioners alike. Taxpayers must ensure that they engage authorized GST Practitioners who are enrolled on the GST portal and have the necessary expertise to handle GST-related matters. GST Practitioners, on the other hand, must comply with the statutory requirements and maintain the highest standards of professional conduct.

For practitioners, key action points include: 1. Ensuring enrollment on the GST portal and maintaining a valid enrollment status. 2. Staying updated with the latest developments in GST law and procedures through continuous education and training. 3. Maintaining confidentiality and integrity in professional practice, adhering to ethical standards. 4. Ensuring compliance with the provisions of the CGST Act and Rules, including those related to record-keeping and representation before GST authorities. 5. Providing advisory services that add value to taxpayers, including guidance on compliance, reconciliations, and representation in audits, assessments, and appeals.

Ultimately, the effectiveness of GST Practitioners in facilitating compliance with GST laws will depend on their professionalism, expertise, and commitment to ethical standards. As the GST regime continues to evolve, the role of GST Practitioners will remain critical in ensuring that taxpayers navigate the complexities of GST law with ease and compliance.

items.title: FCRA Amendment Rules 2026: 10 Major Compliance Changes
items.summary: Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Rules, 2026 introduce key changes to FCRA compliance, including key functionaries, State-wise registration, expanded disclosures, Form FC-3BB, and stricter governance requirements effective from 22 June 2026. The post FCRA Amendment Rules 2026: 10 Major Compliance Changes appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act, 2010, (hereinafter referred to as "FCRA") is a legislation aimed at regulating the acceptance and utilization of foreign contributions by certain individuals, associations, and companies. The primary objective of the FCRA is to ensure that foreign contributions are not utilized for any activities detrimental to the national interest. The Act is supplemented by the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Rules, 2011, which provide the procedural framework for the implementation of the FCRA. Section 48 of the FCRA empowers the Central Government to make rules for carrying out the provisions of the Act. In exercise of this power, the Central Government has notified the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Rules, 2026, which introduce significant changes to the FCRA compliance framework.

Under Section 6 of the FCRA, certain entities, such as political parties, election candidates, and news media, are prohibited from accepting foreign contributions. However, other entities, such as NGOs and charitable organizations, are permitted to accept foreign contributions, subject to certain conditions and restrictions. The FCRA also requires entities accepting foreign contributions to register with the Central Government and to file annual returns. Section 18 of the FCRA provides for the registration of certain entities, while Section 19 mandates the filing of annual returns. The Amendment Rules, 2026, have introduced significant changes to these provisions, which are discussed in detail below.

The FCRA has been amended from time to time to strengthen its provisions and to ensure that foreign contributions are not misutilized. For instance, the Finance Act, 2020, introduced amendments to Section 11 of the FCRA, which deals with the surrender of the certificate of registration. The Amendment Rules, 2026, are another step in this direction, aimed at further streamlining the FCRA framework and ensuring that foreign contributions are utilized for genuine charitable purposes.

The Development in Detail

The Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Rules, 2026, were notified on 22 June 2026, and they introduce 10 major compliance changes to the FCRA framework. One of the key changes is the introduction of a new Form FC-3BB, which is required to be filed by entities seeking prior approval for the transfer of foreign contributions to other registered entities. This change is aimed at increasing transparency and accountability in the utilization of foreign contributions. Another significant change is the expansion of disclosures required to be made by entities accepting foreign contributions, which includes details of their key functionaries and their relationships with other entities.

The Amendment Rules, 2026, also introduce stricter governance requirements for entities accepting foreign contributions. For instance, entities are now required to maintain separate accounts for foreign contributions and to utilize these contributions only for the purposes specified in their registration certificates. The Rules also introduce new provisions for the registration of entities, which include the requirement for State-wise registration and the submission of additional documentation. These changes are aimed at strengthening the regulatory framework and preventing the misutilization of foreign contributions.

The introduction of the Amendment Rules, 2026, is a significant development in the context of the FCRA, as it demonstrates the Central Government's commitment to regulating foreign contributions and ensuring that they are utilized for genuine charitable purposes. The Rules are also consistent with the Supreme Court's decision in the case of Association for Democratic Reforms vs. Union of India, which emphasized the need for transparency and accountability in the acceptance and utilization of foreign contributions.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Amendment Rules, 2026, introduce several key provisions that have a significant impact on entities accepting foreign contributions. For instance, the requirement for State-wise registration is aimed at increasing transparency and accountability, as it enables the State Governments to monitor the activities of entities accepting foreign contributions. The introduction of Form FC-3BB is also a significant development, as it requires entities to disclose details of their key functionaries and their relationships with other entities. This change is aimed at preventing the utilization of foreign contributions for any activities detrimental to the national interest.

The expansion of disclosures required to be made by entities accepting foreign contributions is another significant provision introduced by the Amendment Rules, 2026. This change is aimed at increasing transparency and accountability, as it enables the Central Government to monitor the activities of entities accepting foreign contributions. The introduction of stricter governance requirements is also a significant development, as it ensures that foreign contributions are utilized only for the purposes specified in the registration certificates of entities.

The Amendment Rules, 2026, have significant implications for entities accepting foreign contributions, as they introduce new compliance requirements and stricter governance standards. Entities must ensure that they comply with these requirements, as non-compliance can result in penal consequences, including the cancellation of their registration certificates. The Rules also have implications for the Central Government, as they demonstrate its commitment to regulating foreign contributions and ensuring that they are utilized for genuine charitable purposes.

Critical Analysis

The Amendment Rules, 2026, are a significant development in the context of the FCRA, as they introduce several key provisions that strengthen the regulatory framework. The introduction of Form FC-3BB and the expansion of disclosures required to be made by entities accepting foreign contributions are significant developments, as they increase transparency and accountability. The stricter governance requirements introduced by the Rules are also a significant development, as they ensure that foreign contributions are utilized only for the purposes specified in the registration certificates of entities.

However, the Amendment Rules, 2026, also raise several concerns, as they introduce new compliance requirements and stricter governance standards. Entities accepting foreign contributions must ensure that they comply with these requirements, as non-compliance can result in penal consequences. The Rules also demonstrate the Central Government's commitment to regulating foreign contributions, which may have implications for the freedom of speech and association guaranteed by Article 19 of the Constitution of India.

The Amendment Rules, 2026, must be analyzed in the context of the Supreme Court's decision in the case of People's Union for Civil Liberties vs. Union of India, which emphasized the need for a balance between the regulation of foreign contributions and the freedom of speech and association. The Rules must be implemented in a manner that ensures this balance, as excessive regulation can have a chilling effect on the activities of entities accepting foreign contributions.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Amendment Rules, 2026, have significant compliance implications for entities accepting foreign contributions. Entities must ensure that they comply with the new requirements introduced by the Rules, including the requirement for State-wise registration and the submission of Form FC-3BB. Entities must also ensure that they maintain separate accounts for foreign contributions and utilize these contributions only for the purposes specified in their registration certificates.

Key takeaways for practitioners include:

  1. Ensure that entities accepting foreign contributions comply with the new requirements introduced by the Amendment Rules, 2026, including the requirement for State-wise registration and the submission of Form FC-3BB.
  2. Conduct a thorough review of the governance structure of entities accepting foreign contributions to ensure that they comply with the stricter governance requirements introduced by the Rules.
  3. Ensure that entities accepting foreign contributions maintain separate accounts for foreign contributions and utilize these contributions only for the purposes specified in their registration certificates.
  4. Provide training to key functionaries of entities accepting foreign contributions on the new compliance requirements introduced by the Amendment Rules, 2026.
  5. Review the registration certificates of entities accepting foreign contributions to ensure that they are valid and that the entities are complying with the conditions specified in the certificates.

Entities accepting foreign contributions must take immediate action to comply with the new requirements introduced by the Amendment Rules, 2026, to avoid penal consequences. Practitioners must also be aware of the compliance implications of the Rules and provide guidance to entities on the new requirements.

items.title: CARO 2020: Why Physical Verification Matters More Than QR Codes
items.summary: Summary: The Companies (Auditor’s Report) Order, 2020 (CARO 2020) does not mandate asset tagging through QR codes, barcodes, or RFID technology. Instead, Clause 3(i)(b) requires the auditor to report on whether Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) have been physically verified by the management at reasonable intervals, whether the frequency of verification is appropriate considering the […] The post CARO 2020: Why Physical Verification Matters More Than QR Codes…
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Companies (Auditor’s Report) Order, 2020 (CARO 2020) is a significant development in the realm of company audits, aiming to enhance transparency and accountability in financial reporting. As per Section 143(11) of the Companies Act, 2013, the auditor's report shall include certain matters as may be specified by the Central Government. In this context, CARO 2020 has been introduced to provide a framework for auditors to report on specific aspects of a company's financial statements. The Order is a critical component of the auditing process, as it enables stakeholders to assess the company's financial health and compliance with regulatory requirements.

From a tax perspective, the CARO 2020 assumes significance, particularly in relation to the verification of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE). Clause 3(i)(b) of the Order requires the auditor to report on whether PPE have been physically verified by the management at reasonable intervals. This provision is crucial, as it ensures that companies maintain accurate records of their assets and prevent potential misstatements in their financial statements. As per Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the method of accounting adopted by a company should be such that it results in a true and fair view of its financial statements. In this context, physical verification of PPE is essential to ensure that the company's financial statements accurately reflect its assets and liabilities.

The importance of physical verification of PPE can also be seen in the context of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime. As per Section 16 of the CGST Act, 2017, a registered person is entitled to claim input tax credit (ITC) on goods or services used in the course of business. However, to claim ITC, the registered person must maintain accurate records of its assets and inventory. Physical verification of PPE is essential to ensure that the company maintains accurate records of its assets and can claim ITC accordingly.

The Development in Detail

CARO 2020 does not mandate asset tagging through QR codes, barcodes, or RFID technology. Instead, the focus is on physical verification of PPE by the management at reasonable intervals. This approach is significant, as it emphasizes the importance of manual verification of assets, rather than relying solely on technology. The Order requires the auditor to report on whether the frequency of verification is appropriate, considering the size, nature, and complexity of the company's operations. This provision ensures that companies adopt a risk-based approach to asset verification, taking into account the specific requirements of their business.

The development is also significant in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic, which has accelerated the adoption of digital technologies in various aspects of business. While technology can enhance the efficiency and accuracy of asset verification, it is not a substitute for physical verification. CARO 2020 recognizes the importance of physical verification, ensuring that companies maintain a balance between technology and manual verification. As per the CBDT Circular No. 8/2020, dated March 30, 2020, the due date for filing income tax returns was extended due to the pandemic. In this context, CARO 2020 provides a framework for companies to maintain accurate records of their assets, despite the challenges posed by the pandemic.

The Order also highlights the importance of internal controls and risk management in asset verification. As per Section 134(5)(e) of the Companies Act, 2013, the Board of Directors of a company shall lay down internal financial controls to be followed by the company. CARO 2020 requires the auditor to report on whether the company has adequate internal financial controls in place, including those related to asset verification. This provision ensures that companies have a robust system of internal controls, which can help prevent misstatements in financial statements and ensure compliance with regulatory requirements.

Key Provisions & Impact

CARO 2020 has significant implications for companies, auditors, and regulators. The Order requires companies to maintain accurate records of their PPE and to physically verify these assets at reasonable intervals. This provision can help prevent misstatements in financial statements and ensure compliance with regulatory requirements. As per Section 217(1) of the Companies Act, 2013, the Board of Directors of a company shall prepare a report on the company's financial performance. CARO 2020 provides a framework for companies to prepare this report, ensuring that it accurately reflects the company's financial position and performance.

The Order also has implications for auditors, who must report on whether the company has complied with the provisions of CARO 2020. As per Section 143(12) of the Companies Act, 2013, the auditor shall make a report to the members of the company on the accounts examined by him. CARO 2020 provides a framework for auditors to report on the company's compliance with regulatory requirements, including those related to asset verification. This provision can help enhance the credibility of financial statements and ensure that stakeholders have access to accurate information about the company's financial performance.

The impact of CARO 2020 can also be seen in the context of the GST regime. As per Section 35(5) of the CGST Act, 2017, every registered person shall maintain records of its assets and inventory. CARO 2020 provides a framework for companies to maintain these records, ensuring that they can claim ITC accurately and comply with GST regulations. This provision can help prevent disputes and litigation related to GST, ensuring that companies can focus on their core business activities.

Critical Analysis

CARO 2020 is a significant development in the realm of company audits, as it emphasizes the importance of physical verification of PPE. The Order recognizes that technology is not a substitute for manual verification, and that companies must adopt a risk-based approach to asset verification. This approach is critical, as it ensures that companies maintain accurate records of their assets and can prevent misstatements in financial statements. As per the Supreme Court judgment in the case of Hindustan Lever Ltd. vs. State of Maharashtra, (2004) 9 SCC 438, the court held that the method of accounting adopted by a company should be such that it results in a true and fair view of its financial statements.

However, CARO 2020 also raises certain challenges for companies and auditors. The Order requires companies to maintain accurate records of their PPE, which can be a time-consuming and resource-intensive process. Additionally, the requirement for physical verification of assets at reasonable intervals can be challenging, particularly for companies with large and complex operations. As per the CBDT Circular No. 17/2020, dated September 29, 2020, the CBDT has provided guidance on the implementation of CARO 2020. This guidance can help companies and auditors navigate the challenges posed by the Order and ensure compliance with regulatory requirements.

Despite these challenges, CARO 2020 is a critical development in the realm of company audits. The Order provides a framework for companies to maintain accurate records of their assets, prevent misstatements in financial statements, and comply with regulatory requirements. As per the report of the Committee on Corporate Governance, constituted by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs, the committee recommended that companies should maintain accurate records of their assets and liabilities. CARO 2020 provides a framework for companies to implement this recommendation, ensuring that they can maintain the trust and confidence of stakeholders.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

CARO 2020 has significant implications for companies, auditors, and regulators. To ensure compliance with the Order, companies must maintain accurate records of their PPE and physically verify these assets at reasonable intervals. The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Companies must maintain accurate records of their PPE, including details of asset location, description, and valuation. This information should be updated regularly to reflect changes in the company's assets and liabilities.

2. Companies must physically verify their PPE at reasonable intervals, taking into account the size, nature, and complexity of their operations. This verification should be done by authorized personnel, and the results should be documented and retained for future reference.

3. Auditors must report on whether the company has complied with the provisions of CARO 2020, including the requirement for physical verification of PPE. This report should be included in the auditor's report, as per Section 143(12) of the Companies Act, 2013.

4. Companies must ensure that they have adequate internal financial controls in place, including those related to asset verification. This includes maintaining accurate records of assets, physically verifying assets at reasonable intervals, and ensuring that authorized personnel are responsible for asset verification.

5. Companies must comply with the GST regulations, including those related to asset verification and ITC claims. This includes maintaining accurate records of assets and inventory, physically verifying assets at reasonable intervals, and ensuring that ITC claims are accurate and compliant with GST regulations.

items.title: Working in India for a Foreign Employer? Here’s How Your Salary Is Taxed
items.summary: Working remotely in India for a foreign employer? Learn where your salary is taxed, how the India–US DTAA applies, advance tax obligations, Foreign Tax Credit, and key compliance requirements for cross-border remote workers. The post Working in India for a Foreign Employer? Here’s How Your Salary Is Taxed appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, governs the taxation of income in India, including income earned by individuals working for foreign employers. Section 5 of the Act provides that income which is deemed to accrue or arise in India shall be included in the total income of the person. Section 9 of the Act deals with the income deemed to accrue or arise in India, which includes income from a business connection in India. The India-US Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement (DTAA) also plays a crucial role in determining the taxability of income earned by individuals working for foreign employers in India.

Section 90 of the Income Tax Act provides that the Central Government may enter into an agreement with the government of any country outside India for the avoidance of double taxation of income. The India-US DTAA is one such agreement, which aims to avoid double taxation and fiscal evasion. The DTAA provides that income which is taxable in one country shall not be taxable in the other country, unless the income is derived from sources in the other country. In the context of remote workers, the DTAA assumes significance in determining the taxability of their income in India.

The concept of "business connection" under Section 9 of the Income Tax Act is also relevant for remote workers. The term "business connection" includes any business activity carried out through a person in India, whether directly or indirectly. If a remote worker is deemed to have a business connection in India, their income may be subject to tax in India, even if their employer is a foreign entity. The CBDT has issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the concept of business connection and its application to remote workers, which are essential for understanding the tax implications of working remotely in India.

The Development in Detail

The development in question relates to the taxation of salary income earned by individuals working remotely in India for foreign employers. The key issue is whether such income is taxable in India and, if so, how it is taxed. The India-US DTAA provides that income from employment is taxable in the country where the employment is exercised, unless the employment is exercised on a ship or aircraft operated in international traffic. In the case of remote workers, the employment is exercised in India, which raises questions about the taxability of their income in India.

Section 192 of the Income Tax Act requires every person responsible for paying any income chargeable under the head "Salaries" to deduct income-tax thereon at the time of payment. However, in the case of foreign employers, the obligation to deduct tax at source may not arise, as the employer may not have a presence in India. In such cases, the individual may be required to pay advance tax on their income, as per Section 208 of the Income Tax Act. The CBDT has issued guidelines to clarify the advance tax obligations of remote workers, which are essential for ensuring compliance with the tax laws.

The Foreign Tax Credit (FTC) regime under Section 91 of the Income Tax Act is also relevant for remote workers. The FTC regime allows taxpayers to claim a credit for foreign taxes paid on income which is also taxable in India. However, the FTC regime is subject to certain conditions and limitations, which must be carefully evaluated by remote workers to ensure that they are eligible for the credit. The CBDT has issued circulars to clarify the FTC regime and its application to remote workers, which are essential for understanding the tax implications of working remotely in India.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provisions of the Income Tax Act and the India-US DTAA have a significant impact on the taxation of salary income earned by remote workers in India. The provisions of Section 9 and Section 5 of the Income Tax Act determine the taxability of such income in India, while the DTAA provides relief from double taxation. The advance tax obligations under Section 208 of the Income Tax Act and the FTC regime under Section 91 of the Act are also crucial for remote workers.

The impact of these provisions is that remote workers may be subject to tax in India on their salary income, even if their employer is a foreign entity. The taxability of such income in India may also depend on the existence of a business connection in India. The FTC regime provides relief from double taxation, but it is subject to certain conditions and limitations. The CBDT guidelines and circulars provide clarity on the tax implications of working remotely in India, which are essential for ensuring compliance with the tax laws.

The provisions of the Income Tax Act and the DTAA also have implications for foreign employers who have employees working remotely in India. Such employers may be required to comply with the tax laws in India, including the obligation to deduct tax at source under Section 192 of the Act. The foreign employer may also be required to obtain a permanent account number (PAN) in India and to file returns of income in India, as per Section 139 of the Act. The CBDT has issued guidelines to clarify the tax obligations of foreign employers, which are essential for ensuring compliance with the tax laws.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the DTAA reveals that the taxation of salary income earned by remote workers in India is complex and nuanced. The existence of a business connection in India is a critical factor in determining the taxability of such income. The advance tax obligations under Section 208 of the Act and the FTC regime under Section 91 of the Act are also crucial for remote workers.

The CBDT guidelines and circulars provide clarity on the tax implications of working remotely in India, but they also highlight the need for careful evaluation of the tax laws and regulations. The tax implications of working remotely in India may vary depending on the specific facts and circumstances of each case, which requires a detailed analysis of the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the DTAA. The concept of "business connection" under Section 9 of the Act is also subject to interpretation, which may lead to disputes and litigation.

The critical analysis also reveals that the taxation of salary income earned by remote workers in India has significant implications for the economy and the tax base. The growth of remote work has created new opportunities for individuals to work from anywhere in the world, but it also raises challenges for tax authorities in terms of ensuring compliance with the tax laws. The CBDT has issued guidelines and circulars to address these challenges, but more needs to be done to provide clarity and certainty on the tax implications of working remotely in India.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of the taxation of salary income earned by remote workers in India are significant. Remote workers must ensure that they comply with the tax laws and regulations in India, including the obligation to pay advance tax under Section 208 of the Act. Foreign employers must also comply with the tax laws in India, including the obligation to deduct tax at source under Section 192 of the Act.

The following are key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Remote workers must evaluate their tax obligations in India and ensure that they comply with the tax laws and regulations. 2. Foreign employers must obtain a PAN in India and file returns of income in India, as per Section 139 of the Act. 3. Remote workers must claim the FTC under Section 91 of the Act, if eligible, to avoid double taxation. 4. Practitioners must carefully evaluate the concept of "business connection" under Section 9 of the Act to determine the taxability of salary income earned by remote workers in India. 5. Practitioners must also ensure that their clients comply with the tax laws and regulations in India, including the obligation to pay advance tax under Section 208 of the Act.

The compliance implications of the taxation of salary income earned by remote workers in India are complex and nuanced, requiring careful evaluation of the tax laws and regulations. Practitioners must stay up-to-date with the latest developments and guidelines issued by the CBDT to ensure that their clients comply with the tax laws and regulations in India.

items.title: Why Can’t I Claim GST ITC on My Car, Staff Lunches & Office Renovation?
items.summary: Why is GST Input Tax Credit denied on cars, staff meals, and office renovation? Learn blocked credit provisions under Section 17(5) of CGST Act, key exceptions and how to avoid costly ITC mistakes. The post Why Can’t I Claim GST ITC on My Car, Staff Lunches & Office Renovation? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), has been a significant overhaul of the indirect tax landscape in India. One of the core principles of GST is the concept of input tax credit (ITC), which allows businesses to claim credit for the tax paid on inputs used in the course of their business. However, the CGST Act also provides for certain restrictions on the availability of ITC, which are crucial for businesses to understand in order to avoid costly mistakes. Section 17(5) of the CGST Act is one such provision that blocks the credit of certain inputs, including motor vehicles, food and beverages, and construction services.

The blocking of ITC under Section 17(5) is based on the principle that certain expenses are not directly related to the business activities of the taxpayer and are, therefore, not eligible for credit. This provision is aimed at preventing businesses from claiming credit on personal expenses or expenses that are not incidental to the business. For instance, the purchase of a car for personal use or the renovation of an office building for the purpose of earning rental income would not be eligible for ITC. However, there are certain exceptions and clarifications that have been provided by the government to ensure that genuine business expenses are not denied credit.

The CGST Act and the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) have issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the scope of Section 17(5) and provide guidance on the eligibility of ITC for different types of expenses. For example, Circular No. 8/8/2017-GST dated October 13, 2017, provides clarification on the eligibility of ITC for motor vehicles and conveyances. Similarly, Notification No. 4/2018-Central Tax (Rate) dated January 25, 2018, provides for the exemption of certain construction services from the purview of Section 17(5).

The Development in Detail

The development in question relates to the denial of ITC on certain expenses, including the purchase of cars, staff meals, and office renovation. The CGST Act provides that ITC shall not be available on the supply of goods or services or both, for personal use or consumption. This means that businesses cannot claim credit for expenses that are not directly related to their business activities. For instance, if a business purchases a car for the personal use of its directors or employees, the ITC on such purchase would be denied.

Similarly, the provision of staff meals or the renovation of an office building for the purpose of earning rental income would also not be eligible for ITC. However, there are certain exceptions to this rule. For example, if a business provides food and beverages to its employees as part of its welfare activities, the ITC on such expenses may be eligible. Similarly, if the renovation of an office building is undertaken to facilitate the business activities of the taxpayer, the ITC on such expenses may be allowed.

The key issue here is to determine the nature and purpose of the expense and to establish whether it is directly related to the business activities of the taxpayer. This requires a careful analysis of the facts and circumstances of each case and a thorough understanding of the provisions of the CGST Act and the relevant circulars and notifications issued by the government. The development in question highlights the need for businesses to carefully evaluate their expenses and to ensure that they are eligible for ITC under the GST regime.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 17(5) of the CGST Act provides for the blocking of ITC on certain expenses, including motor vehicles, food and beverages, and construction services. However, there are certain exceptions to this rule. For example, Clause (a) of Section 17(5) provides that ITC shall not be available on the supply of goods or services or both, for personal use or consumption. On the other hand, Clause (b) provides that ITC shall be available on the supply of goods or services or both, for use in the course of furtherance of business.

The CBIC has also issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the scope of Section 17(5) and to provide guidance on the eligibility of ITC for different types of expenses. For instance, Circular No. 8/8/2017-GST dated October 13, 2017, provides clarification on the eligibility of ITC for motor vehicles and conveyances. Similarly, Notification No. 4/2018-Central Tax (Rate) dated January 25, 2018, provides for the exemption of certain construction services from the purview of Section 17(5).

The key provision here is to understand the distinction between personal use and business use and to establish whether the expense is directly related to the business activities of the taxpayer. This requires a careful analysis of the facts and circumstances of each case and a thorough understanding of the provisions of the CGST Act and the relevant circulars and notifications issued by the government. The clarifications provided by the government are aimed at ensuring that genuine business expenses are not denied credit and that the GST regime is implemented in a fair and equitable manner.

Critical Analysis

The blocking of ITC on certain expenses under Section 17(5) of the CGST Act is a critical aspect of the GST regime. The provision is aimed at preventing businesses from claiming credit on personal expenses or expenses that are not incidental to the business. However, the provision also raises certain complexities and challenges for businesses, particularly in relation to the distinction between personal use and business use.

The critical issue here is to determine the nature and purpose of the expense and to establish whether it is directly related to the business activities of the taxpayer. This requires a careful analysis of the facts and circumstances of each case and a thorough understanding of the provisions of the CGST Act and the relevant circulars and notifications issued by the government. The development in question highlights the need for businesses to carefully evaluate their expenses and to ensure that they are eligible for ITC under the GST regime.

The critical analysis of the provision also reveals that the blocking of ITC on certain expenses is not absolute and that there are certain exceptions to this rule. For example, the provision of staff meals or the renovation of an office building for the purpose of facilitating the business activities of the taxpayer may be eligible for ITC. Therefore, businesses need to carefully evaluate their expenses and to ensure that they are eligible for ITC under the GST regime.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The blocking of ITC on certain expenses under Section 17(5) of the CGST Act has significant compliance implications for businesses. The key issue here is to ensure that businesses carefully evaluate their expenses and to establish whether they are eligible for ITC under the GST regime. The following are some action points for practitioners:

1. Carefully evaluate the nature and purpose of each expense to determine whether it is directly related to the business activities of the taxpayer.

2. Ensure that the expense is not for personal use or consumption, as ITC shall not be available on such expenses.

3. Establish whether the expense is eligible for ITC under the GST regime, taking into account the provisions of Section 17(5) and the relevant circulars and notifications issued by the government.

4. Maintain proper documentation to support the claim for ITC, including invoices, receipts, and other relevant documents.

5. Regularly review and update the accounting and billing systems to ensure that they are aligned with the GST regime and that the business is taking advantage of all eligible ITC.

items.title: GST on Advance for Services: Is Tax Payable Before Work Begins?
items.summary: Is GST payable on advances received for services before work begins? Learn the time of supply rules, GST treatment of service advances, receipt vouchers, refund vouchers, and why advances for goods are treated differently. The post GST on Advance for Services: Is Tax Payable Before Work Begins? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, as enacted under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act), has brought about a paradigm shift in the indirect tax landscape of India. One of the critical aspects of GST is the concept of "time of supply," which determines when a supply of goods or services is deemed to have been made for tax purposes. Section 12 of the CGST Act deals with the time of supply of services, while Section 13 pertains to the time of supply of goods. Understanding these provisions is crucial for businesses to comply with GST regulations, especially when it comes to advances received for services before the actual work begins.

Section 2(31) of the CGST Act defines "consideration" in a broad manner to include any payment made or to be made, whether in money or otherwise, in respect of, in response to, or for the inducement of, the supply of goods or services or both, whether by the recipient or by any other person. This definition is significant when considering the GST implications of advances received for services. The GST Council and the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) have issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the GST treatment of such advances, providing much-needed guidance to taxpayers and practitioners.

The concept of "supply" under GST is also pivotal. As per Section 3 of the CGST Act, the supply of services is deemed to have been made if the consideration is received before the provision of such services. This implies that GST may be payable on advances received for services before the work begins, based on the time of supply rules. However, the specific treatment of such advances, including the issuance of receipt vouchers and refund vouchers, requires careful consideration of the GST laws and regulations.

The Development in Detail

The GST treatment of advances received for services has been a subject of debate among taxpayers and practitioners. The key issue is whether GST is payable on such advances before the actual supply of services. Section 12(2)(a) of the CGST Act states that the time of supply of services shall be the earliest of the date of issue of invoice or the date of receipt of payment, whichever is earlier. This provision suggests that GST may be payable on advances received for services, as the receipt of payment is considered the time of supply.

However, the GST laws also provide for the issuance of receipt vouchers and refund vouchers in respect of advances received for services. As per Section 31(3)(d) of the CGST Act, a receipt voucher is required to be issued by the supplier for every advance payment received by him. Similarly, a refund voucher is to be issued in case of refund of such advances. These provisions are critical in determining the GST treatment of advances received for services, as they provide a mechanism for accounting for such advances and ensuring compliance with GST regulations.

In contrast, advances received for goods are treated differently under GST. Section 12(2)(b) of the CGST Act provides that the time of supply of goods shall be the earliest of the date of issue of invoice or the date of receipt of payment, whichever is earlier. However, the GST laws also provide for the issuance of tax invoices in respect of advances received for goods, as per Section 31(2) of the CGST Act. This highlights the distinction in the GST treatment of advances received for goods and services, which is essential for businesses to understand and comply with GST regulations.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The CBIC has issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the GST treatment of advances received for services. For instance, Circular No. 66/40/2018-GST, dated September 26, 2018, provides guidance on the issuance of receipt vouchers and refund vouchers in respect of advances received for services. The circular clarifies that the receipt voucher should contain the details of the advance payment received, the rate of tax, and the amount of tax payable. Similarly, the refund voucher should contain the details of the refund, including the amount of refund and the rate of tax.

Section 15 of the CGST Act deals with the value of supply, which is critical in determining the GST liability on advances received for services. The section provides that the value of supply shall be the transaction value, which is the price actually paid or payable for the supply of goods or services or both. In the case of advances received for services, the transaction value would include the amount of advance payment received, which would be subject to GST.

The GST laws also provide for the concept of "pure agent" services, as per Section 2(105) of the CGST Act. In cases where the supplier is acting as a pure agent, the GST treatment of advances received for services may be different. The supplier would be required to issue a receipt voucher and account for the advance payment received, but the GST liability would depend on the specific circumstances of the case, including the nature of the services provided and the terms of the contract.

Critical Analysis

The GST treatment of advances received for services has significant implications for businesses, particularly in terms of cash flow and compliance. The requirement to pay GST on advances received for services before the actual work begins can have a substantial impact on the liquidity of businesses, especially small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). On the other hand, the provisions for receipt vouchers and refund vouchers provide a mechanism for accounting for such advances and ensuring compliance with GST regulations.

The distinction in the GST treatment of advances received for goods and services is also noteworthy. While advances received for goods are subject to GST at the time of receipt, advances received for services are subject to GST at the time of receipt or when the invoice is issued, whichever is earlier. This highlights the complexity of GST laws and the need for businesses to carefully consider the implications of advances received for both goods and services.

The GST Council and the CBIC have taken steps to clarify the GST treatment of advances received for services, but further guidance may be necessary to address the concerns of taxpayers and practitioners. The issuance of circulars and notifications has provided much-needed clarity, but the GST laws and regulations are subject to change, and businesses must remain vigilant to ensure compliance and avoid any adverse consequences.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The GST treatment of advances received for services has significant compliance implications for businesses. To ensure compliance with GST regulations, businesses should carefully consider the following action points:

1. Issuance of receipt vouchers: Businesses should ensure that receipt vouchers are issued for all advances received for services, as per Section 31(3)(d) of the CGST Act. The receipt voucher should contain the details of the advance payment received, the rate of tax, and the amount of tax payable.

2. Accounting for advance payments: Businesses should account for advance payments received for services in their books of accounts and ensure that the GST liability is properly recorded. This includes maintaining accurate records of all advances received, including the date of receipt, the amount, and the rate of tax.

3. Payment of GST: Businesses should pay GST on advances received for services, as per Section 12(2)(a) of the CGST Act. The GST liability should be paid at the time of receipt of the advance payment or when the invoice is issued, whichever is earlier.

4. Refund of advances: In case of refund of advances received for services, businesses should issue a refund voucher, as per Section 31(3)(d) of the CGST Act. The refund voucher should contain the details of the refund, including the amount of refund and the rate of tax.

5. Regular review of GST compliance: Businesses should regularly review their GST compliance to ensure that all advances received for services are properly accounted for and that the GST liability is paid in a timely manner. This includes maintaining accurate records, filing GST returns, and paying GST liabilities on time.

items.title: I Moved Abroad Mid-Year – Am I an NRI for Tax This Year, or Not?
items.summary: Confused about your NRI status? Learn how the 182-day rule under Section 6 determines residential status, when foreign salary is taxable in India, and why your departure date can significantly affect your tax liability. The post I Moved Abroad Mid-Year – Am I an NRI for Tax This Year, or Not? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The determination of residential status is a crucial aspect of Indian income tax law, as it has a direct impact on an individual's tax liability. The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides the framework for determining an individual's residential status, which is essential for ascertaining the scope of their taxable income in India. Section 6 of the Act lays down the conditions for determining an individual's residential status, which is categorized into three types: Resident, Non-Resident (NRI), and Resident but Not Ordinarily Resident (RNOR). The 182-day rule, as prescribed under Section 6, plays a significant role in determining an individual's residential status.

According to Section 6, an individual is considered a resident in India if they meet certain conditions, including being physically present in India for at least 182 days in the previous year. However, if an individual leaves India during the year, their residential status may change, affecting their tax liability. The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the residential status of individuals who leave India during the year. For instance, Circular No. 13 of 2020 provides guidance on the residential status of individuals who are required to leave India due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

The concept of residential status is closely linked to the taxation of foreign income. Section 9 of the Income Tax Act deals with the income deemed to be accrued or arisen in India, including income from foreign sources. However, Section 10 provides exemptions for certain types of income, such as income earned by NRIs. The distinction between resident and non-resident status is crucial, as it affects the applicability of these exemptions and the overall tax liability of an individual.

The Development in Detail

The recent development regarding the determination of NRI status for individuals who move abroad mid-year has sparked confusion among taxpayers. The 182-day rule, as mentioned earlier, is the primary criterion for determining an individual's residential status. However, the timing of an individual's departure from India can significantly impact their tax liability. If an individual leaves India during the year, their residential status may change, and they may be considered an NRI for tax purposes.

According to Section 6, an individual who is in India for less than 182 days in the previous year is considered an NRI. However, if an individual is in India for 182 days or more, they are considered a resident, unless they meet certain conditions, such as being a citizen of India or a person of Indian origin. The CBDT has also clarified that an individual's residential status is determined on a previous year basis, which means that their status for the current year is determined based on their stay in India during the previous year.

The determination of NRI status is also affected by the concept of "deemed residence" under Section 6. An individual who is deemed to be a resident in India, despite being physically present in India for less than 182 days, may still be subject to tax in India on their global income. This concept is crucial for individuals who have income from foreign sources, as it may impact their tax liability in India.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision in this context is Section 6 of the Income Tax Act, which lays down the conditions for determining an individual's residential status. The 182-day rule is the primary criterion for determining an individual's residential status, and the timing of an individual's departure from India can significantly impact their tax liability. The CBDT's circulars and guidelines provide additional clarity on the residential status of individuals who leave India during the year.

The impact of the 182-day rule on an individual's tax liability cannot be overstated. If an individual is considered an NRI, they are only subject to tax in India on their Indian-sourced income. However, if they are considered a resident, they are subject to tax in India on their global income, including income from foreign sources. This distinction can result in significant tax savings for individuals who are able to claim NRI status.

Furthermore, the concept of "foreign salary" is also relevant in this context. Section 15 of the Income Tax Act deals with the taxation of foreign salary, which is considered taxable in India if it is received in India or is deemed to be received in India. However, if an individual is considered an NRI, their foreign salary may not be subject to tax in India, unless it is received in India or is deemed to be received in India.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the 182-day rule and its impact on an individual's tax liability reveals that the timing of an individual's departure from India can have significant consequences. If an individual leaves India during the year, their residential status may change, and they may be considered an NRI for tax purposes. This can result in significant tax savings, as NRIs are only subject to tax in India on their Indian-sourced income.

However, the CBDT's circulars and guidelines also provide that an individual's residential status is determined on a previous year basis, which means that their status for the current year is determined based on their stay in India during the previous year. This can create complexities for individuals who leave India during the year, as their residential status may change, but their tax liability for the current year may still be determined based on their previous year's status.

Furthermore, the concept of "deemed residence" under Section 6 can also create complexities for individuals who are deemed to be residents in India, despite being physically present in India for less than 182 days. This concept can result in an individual being subject to tax in India on their global income, even if they are not physically present in India for the entire year.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of the 182-day rule and the determination of NRI status are significant. Individuals who leave India during the year must carefully consider their residential status and the impact on their tax liability. The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Determine the individual's residential status based on the 182-day rule and the CBDT's circulars and guidelines.

2. Consider the timing of the individual's departure from India and its impact on their tax liability.

3. Evaluate the individual's foreign-sourced income and its tax implications in India.

4. Ensure that the individual is in compliance with the tax filing requirements in India, including the filing of Form 67 and the payment of advance tax.

5. Consider seeking professional advice to ensure that the individual's tax liability is minimized and that they are in compliance with the tax laws in India.

items.title: Why Every Business Needs Product Costing Advice from a Cost Accountant
items.summary: Summary: Accurate product costing is essential for sustainable profitability, yet many businesses continue to price products and services without knowing their true cost. A qualified Cost and Management Accountant (CMA) helps organizations determine the actual cost of production or service delivery by analysing material, labour, overheads, capacity utilisation, inventory, quality costs, and other cost components. Professional […] The post Why Every Business Needs…
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The importance of accurate product costing in determining the sustainability of a business's profitability cannot be overstated. As per Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, the valuation of inventory is a critical aspect of computing business income. The section mandates that the valuation of inventory shall be made at the actual cost of the inventory or net realizable value, whichever is lower.
